request.question: How many venues are involved in the main tournament which is part of the English Cricket Team's 2017-2018 schedule?

title: 2017–18 Ashes series
Betty Archdale

English cricket team in Australia in 2017–18
sentences: The 2017–18 Ashes series is a forthcoming series of Test cricket matches to be contested between England and Australia for The Ashes.
The series will be played at five venues across Australia between 23 November 2017 and 8 January 2018.
Helen Elizabeth "Betty" Archdale (21 August 1907 – 11 January 2000) was an educationalist and cricketer.
She was a captain of the English women's cricket team in 1934 and 1935.
In 1934/35 she led the first English cricket team to tour Australia and New Zealand, the result of which was a 2-0 victory over Australia.
This tour did much both to raise the status of women's cricket and to heal some of the damage done to Anglo-Australian cricket relations by bodyline two years earlier.
Centenary Test refers to two matches of Test cricket played between the English cricket team and the Australian cricket team, the first in 1977 and the second in 1980.
These matches were played to mark the 100th anniversaries of the first Test cricket matches played in Australia (1877) and in England (1880) respectively.
Neither match was played for The Ashes.
Robert "Bob" Elliott Storey Wyatt (2 May 1901, Milford Heath House, Surrey, England – 20 April 1995, Treliske, Truro, Cornwall) was an English cricket player.
He played for Warwickshire, Worcestershire, and the English cricket team.
Before the beginning of the 2005 English cricket season began, a resurgent English cricket team had won four Test series in a row, going unbeaten through the 2004 calendar year.
The start of the international season saw England defeat Bangladesh 2–0 in their two-match series, winning both Tests by an innings.
This was followed by a tri-nations one-day tournament that also featured Australia.
However, all eyes were on the Ashes.
Australia still started as favourites, but England, for the first time in years, were expected to put up a real challenge to Aussie supremacy.
The English cricket team toured India during February, March and April 2006.

The English cricket team was aspiring to maintain the form that took them to second place in the ICC Test Championship before their disastrous spell against Pakistan, and which helped win the 2005 Ashes series at home to Australia.

This goal was substantially hindered by the usual stomach complaints which nearly always dog the English team in the opening weeks of Indian tours, and a recurrence of an injury to the captain Michael Vaughan; the swing bowler Simon Jones and the absence of Ashley Giles who missed the tour for an operation.

As well as this, stand-in captain Marcus Trescothick flew home for "personal reasons", not wishing to divulge further, leaving Andrew Flintoff, who missed the birth of his son, to take on the title of skipper for the first time having to captain two maiden international cricketers on the English side: Alastair Cook and Monty Panesar as well as Shanthakumaran Sreesanth, Piyush Chawla and Munaf Patel for the home team.

International cricket played in the 2006 cricket season is defined as matches scheduled between May and August 2006 in all cricketing countries, as well as all international matches scheduled for the 2006 English cricket season.
Matches between January and April are defined as belonging to the 2005–06 season, while matches between September and December are defined as the 2006–07 season.

The main matches in this period were played in England, as this was in the middle of the English cricket season, but the third edition of the ICC Intercontinental Cup is defined as a part of the 2006 season, even though the tournament will stretch into February 2007, and three A teams are also scheduled to tour the northwestern part of Australia in June and July 2006.
The Barmy Army is a semi-organised group of English cricket fans which arranges touring parties for some of its members to follow the English cricket team on overseas tours.
The name is also applied to followers of the team who join in with match day activities in the crowd, but do not necessarily travel as part of an organised tour.
The 2004 English cricket season saw the English cricket team record a 4-0 Test series whitewash over the West Indies and a comfortable 3-0 win over New Zealand.
Their one-day form was sporadic, however.
In the Natwest Trophy, they failed to make the final, which saw New Zealand defeat the West Indies by 107 runs.
In the Natwest Challenge, they beat India 2-1.
In domestic cricket, Warwickshire won the County Championship.
The England cricket team is scheduled to tour Australia between November 2017 and February 2018 to play five Tests and five One Day Internationals (ODIs).
They will also take part in a three nation Twenty20 International (T20I) tournament, along with New Zealand, who will co-host the tournament along with Australia.
The Test series will form part of the 2017–18 Ashes series.
In May 2017, it was confirmed that the WACA Ground would host the Test and ODI in Perth, as the planned new ground was unable to open in time.

title: English cricket team in Australia in 2017–18
2017–18 Ashes series

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question` and `passages`, produce the single field `summary`.

Goal: compress the grounded first-hop evidence into a short intermediate state that makes it easy for the next-hop agent to (a) see what facts are present per candidate/entity, (b) see what is ambiguous or missing, and (c) know 1–4 prioritized next-hop retrieval targets or concise queries. This module does not produce the final answer unless the provided passages already unambiguously contain every fact required to resolve the question's relation for every argument.

Procedure:
1) Decide whether the question's relation is semantically complete given the supplied passages. Treat the hop as INCOMPLETE if any of the following hold: a) the relation requires combining or comparing facts across pages (e.g., comparing birth dates, publication/founding years, ordinal ranks, confirming a named director among candidates) and the passages do not supply each required fact unambiguously for all relevant arguments; b) any relation argument is ambiguous or has multiple candidate pages or mentions in the passages; c) an expected candidate page or explicit fact for an argument is missing (e.g., only one side of a comparison is present). Do not trigger INCOMPLETE solely from surface tokens (years, digits) — base the decision on whether the relation's arguments are unambiguously resolved.

2) If the hop is COMPLETE (all required facts to resolve the relation for every argument are present unambiguously in the passages): produce a concise derived result in `summary`. This may be a short phrase or sentence that states the necessary fact(s) needed by the pipeline. Keep it minimal and grounded in the passages; you may include a 1–2 token citation (passage title) if helpful.

3) If the hop is INCOMPLETE or AMBIGUOUS: do NOT assert a final answer or perform the final comparison/selection. Instead emit a short structured report in `summary` containing these labeled elements (prefer bullets or a compact paragraph with explicit labels). Keep all text neutral and non-assertive.

  - evidence: For each named candidate/argument mentioned in the question or passages, list the candidate label and the exact passage title(s) that mention it plus a very short quoted snippet or explicit fact token found there (1–12 words). If a candidate is not mentioned, say so briefly.

  - ambiguities_or_missing: Explicitly name any ambiguities (e.g., multiple candidates for the same name, multiple pages that could be the intended entity) and any missing expected pages or facts.

  - missing_facts_or_bridge: State exactly which fact(s) or disambiguation is needed to complete the relation (e.g., "birth date for Person A", "publication year of Work B", "director credit for Film C"). Be as specific and minimal as possible.

  - retrieval_direction: Provide 1–4 prioritized next-hop targets or concise queries to resolve the missing facts/ambiguities. Prefer explicit page titles when available (exact if known), otherwise provide short search-style queries (few words) or a clear description of the document to retrieve. Order targets by priority.

Formatting and style constraints (apply to both COMPLETE and INCOMPLETE cases):
- The output must be a single `summary` field only. Do not emit other fields.
- In INCOMPLETE reports, use neutral, non-assertive phrasing. Do not produce final comparisons, numeric answers, or definitive selections (e.g., avoid "X is the oldest" or "Answer: 3").
- Keep the report compact and actionable (short labeled bullets or one concise paragraph). Avoid long prose.
- It is allowed and encouraged to quote short factual snippets from passages and cite passage titles to show grounding.
- Do not defer or mark INCOMPLETE when the passages already unambiguously provide all facts for the relation and its arguments.

Produce the `summary` content that follows these rules.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the single field `query`.

Goal
- Produce exactly one compact, retrieval-intent query suitable for a second-hop search that will recover the missing bridging evidence needed to answer the question. Do not produce the final answer in `query`.

Semantic decision rule (when to emit a retrieval-intent query)
- If summary_1 does NOT assert a confirmed linking entity or verified bridging fact needed to answer the question, you MUST emit exactly one compact retrieval-intent query. "Does not assert a confirmed bridge" includes short or candidate-only summaries (e.g., a single named entity, a quoted candidate title, or numeric anchors that are not verified) and any case where the visible anchors do not unambiguously identify the needed bridge.
- If summary_1 already names a confirmed bridge (a clearly identified, verified linking entity or unambiguous metadata that directly connects the question's pieces), you may produce a compact query to fetch supporting evidence, but do NOT produce an answer in the `query` field.
- If summary_1 explicitly states that the bridge cannot be recovered automatically (extreme ambiguity or user-local context requiring clarification), do NOT force a misleading retrieval query. In that case, set `query` to the single short token BRIDGE_UNRECOVERABLE (all caps, no punctuation) so downstream steps can prompt the user for clarification. Do not invent facts.

Query construction rules
- Output exactly one compact line of text as `query`. Keep it short and retrieval-focused (few keywords, no full sentences). Use visible anchors from `question` and `summary_1` and minimal retrieval keywords that express the missing relation (e.g., anchor1 anchor2 which artist recorded both; AlbumTitle album artist genre; 1991–92 NHL season regular season).
- Do not include the final answer, assertions, or user-facing instructions in the `query` field. Avoid polite phrasing, questions to the user, or explanatory text. Prefer noun phrases and relation keywords.
- If your natural impulse would be to write a declarative answer or a clarification sentence in `query`, instead convert that output into a compact retrieval-intent query that targets the missing bridge.
- Preserve semantic fidelity: base the query only on visible anchors and relations in `question` and `summary_1`. Do not invent hidden identifiers or assume unseen facts.

Format contract
- Return exactly one field named `query` whose value is a single short string. If unrecoverable as described above, set `query` to BRIDGE_UNRECOVERABLE.

Examples of disallowed outputs for `query`
- A declarative answer (e.g., "The artist was X.")
- A user-facing clarification sentence (e.g., "Do you mean X or Y?") unless summary_1 explicitly requires clarification — in that case use BRIDGE_UNRECOVERABLE.

Behavioral constraints
- Do not use surface heuristics (token count, punctuation, or quotation marks) as the trigger; apply the semantic rule above.
- Emit only one compact retrieval string when the bridge is missing; do not emit multiple queries or long explanations.

Inputs available: `question`, `summary_1`. Output required: single field `query`.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`. The `summary` must be a concise, post-retrieval integration of the evidence that preserves the facts needed by the final answer. Follow these rules when composing `summary`:

- Purpose: Integrate the first-hop `context` with the newly retrieved `passages` into a grounded state that retains or resolves the earlier bridge only when the second-hop passages supply relation- or attribute-facts that are necessary to answer or disambiguate the `question` (for example: partnership/membership links, joint-award partners indicated by 'with', or requested biographical qualifiers such as nationality, birthdate, current team/status).

- When to append supporting sentence(s): Only append short passage-grounded supporting sentence(s) when the question's semantic intent requires relation or attribute facts to answer or disambiguate. In those cases, produce a compact integrated summary that (a) preserves the concise primary answer or canonical label/token chosen from `context`, and (b) appends one short supporting sentence per relevant entity (or one joint sentence for explicit partnerships) that is directly supported by the supplied `passages` and identity-aligned to the entity referenced in `context`.

- When not to append: Do not append supporting sentences and instead return only the canonical label/token when any of the following apply: the question expects a normalized identifier or single token (examples: binary/multiple-choice label selection between named options, identification-only questions, or explicit token-extraction such as 'middle name'); or the `context` already contains a full, passage-consistent, multi-clause integrated answer. These are semantic exceptions — do not append merely because an answer in `context` is short.

- Identity alignment and verification: Before appending, confirm that the fact in `passages` refers to the same entity referenced in `context` (match names, common aliases, roles, dates, or other corroborating attributes). If identity is ambiguous or unsupported, refrain from appending and preserve uncertainty instead.

- Style and content constraints: Keep output concise. If appending, limit `summary` to 1–3 sentences total (the primary concise answer plus at most one short supporting sentence per relevant entity or one joint supporting sentence). Avoid inventing facts, avoid long verbatim quotations from `passages`, and avoid name-collision errors. Do not change which label or name is selected by the existing answer-selection logic.

- If there is insufficient information in `context` and `passages` to resolve the needed relation/attribute, summarize the uncertainty succinctly rather than asserting unsupported facts.

Produce only the `summary` field as the integrated output string.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Use only summary_1 and summary_2 as evidence. Treat summary_2 as the later, integrated evidence and prioritize it when reconciling information; use summary_1 only as the earlier, pre-second-hop context. Do not assume the final state is unresolved solely because summary_1 contains bridging or retrieval-oriented language.

Produce exactly one field, answer, whose value is the minimal final answer string that is fully supported by the visible summaries. The answer field must contain only that minimal plain-text string and nothing else (no explanation, no surrounding quotes, no JSON wrappers, no debugging or transport artifacts).

Decide whether the question's intent is binary (a truth-value / yes-no question) or non-binary (asking for a name, title, date, place, numeric count, selection, or other factual value). Make this decision semantically (based on the question's meaning), not by surface heuristics like token counts or punctuation.

- If the question's intent is binary AND both summary_1 and summary_2 unambiguously agree on the same truth-value, emit exactly one lowercase token: either yes or no (and nothing else) as the answer value.

- For all other question intents (non-binary), emit the minimal factual string supported by the summaries exactly as needed: preserve multi-token values, necessary punctuation, full date formats, and place formats (for example, full names, "City, State", or "Month Day, Year"), and do not truncate these to a single token.

If the summaries conflict, are ambiguous, or do not jointly support an unambiguous minimal answer, do not fabricate or over-assert; follow the system's established fallback behavior for ambiguous or unsupported questions.

Always forbid emitting serialized queries, JSON/dict-like structures, internal-debug wrappers, or other transport artifacts as the final answer. The answer must be plain text only.

Make all judgments about minimality and agreement based on the semantic content of summary_1 and summary_2, with priority to summary_2 when they differ.

Produce the answer value exactly as specified above in the answer field.

response.hop1_query: How many venues are involved in the main tournament which is part of the English Cricket Team's 2017-2018 schedule?
hop1_docs: English cricket team in Australia in 2017–18 | The England cricket team is scheduled to tour Australia between November 2017 and February 2018 to play five Tests and five One Day Internationals (ODIs).  They will also take part in a three nation Twenty20 International (T20I) tournament, along with New Zealand, who will co-host the tournament along with Australia.  The Test series will form part of the 2017–18 Ashes series.  In May 2017, it was confirmed that the WACA Ground would host the Test and ODI in Perth, as the planned new ground was unable to open in time.

2017–18 Regional Super50 | The 2017–18 Regional Super50 is scheduled to be the 44th edition of the Regional Super50, the domestic limited-overs cricket competition for the countries of the West Indies Cricket Board (WICB).  The tournament is scheduled to be held in early 2018, with the English county sides Kent and Hampshire being invited to take part in the competition.  Kent took part in the previous edition of the tournament.  Barbados are the defending champions.

2018 ICC World Cricket League Division Four | The 2018 ICC World Cricket League Division Four is an international cricket tournament that is scheduled to take place during mid-2018.  It will form part of the 2017–22 cycle of the World Cricket League (WCL) which determines the qualification for the 2023 Cricket World Cup.

2018 ICC World Cricket League Division Two | 2018 ICC World Cricket League Division Two is a cricket tournament that is scheduled to take place in 2018.  Oman and Canada were the top two teams in the 2017 ICC World Cricket League Division Three tournament in Uganda and were promoted as a result.  They will be joined by the bottom four teams from the ongoing 2015–17 ICC World Cricket League Championship to determine the final two spots in the 2018 Cricket World Cup Qualifier.

2017–18 Pac-12 Conference men's basketball season | The 2017–18 Pac-12 Conference men's basketball season begins with practices in October 2017 followed by the 2017–18 NCAA Division I men's basketball season in November 10, 2017 with the conference schedule will begin on December 27, 2017.  The 2018 Pac-12 Tournament in March 2018 at the T-Mobile Arena in Paradise, Nevada.  Due to the Pac-12's use of that venue for their conference tournament, the Pac-12 Tournament will take place one week earlier than usual, ending the week before Selection Sunday.  This could result in teams having nearly two weeks off before the 2018 NCAA tournament.  As a result, it is anticipated that the Pac-12 regular season will begin in late-December.  Pac-12 teams' first Pac-12 game would likely be on the weekend of December, 2017 with a second game on December , 2017.  Coaches have requested that Pac-12 game be scheduled between Christmas and New Year's Day, accordingly each team will play two conference games in early December before finishing non-conference play.

2017–18 Pro50 Championship | The 2017–18 Pro50 Championship is scheduled to be the fifteenth edition of the Pro50 Championship, a List A cricket tournament in Zimbabwe.  The competition will run from December 2017 to March 2018 and will include a new team, the Rising Stars, along with the existing four teams.  Matabeleland Tuskers are the defending champions.

2018 ICC Women's World Twenty20 | The 2018 ICC Women's World Twenty20 will be hosted in the West Indies from 2 to 25 November 2018, during the 2018–19 international cricket season.  It will be the sixth edition of the ICC Women's World Twenty20, and the second hosted by the West Indies (after the 2010 edition).  The tournament was awarded to the West Indies Cricket Board (WICB) at the 2013 annual conference of the International Cricket Council (ICC).  The tournament's dates were confirmed at an ICC board meeting in January 2015.  In February 2017, the ICC confirmed that this would be the first T20 tournament that uses the Umpire Decision Review System, with one review per side.  The qualifier tournament for the competition is scheduled to be held in late 2017 or early 2018.

response.summary_1:
- evidence:
  - Tri‑nation T20I tournament (England, New Zealand, Australia): passage [1] — "three nation Twenty20 International (T20I) tournament" and "co‑host ... New Zealand ... Australia."
  - England tour (Tests/ODIs): passage [1] — "five Tests and five One Day Internationals (ODIs)."
  - Regional Super50 (West Indies domestic): passage [2] — "2017–18 Regional Super50 ... Kent and Hampshire invited."
  - 2018 ICC Women's World Twenty20: passage [7] — "hosted in the West Indies from 2 to 25 November 2018."
  - None of the above passages give a venue count for the tournament(s).

- ambiguities_or_missing:
  - Unclear which "main tournament" the question refers to (most likely the tri‑nation T20I mentioned in passage [1], but not explicit).
  - No passage supplies the number of venues used by that tournament.

- missing_facts_or_bridge:
  - Confirmation of which tournament is the "main tournament" in England's 2017–18 schedule (if not the tri‑nation T20I).
  - The number of venues (list or count) used in that tournament.

- retrieval_direction (priority):
  1. Tournament page for the tri‑nation T20I involving England, New Zealand and Australia in 2017–18 (search/query: "2017–18 England New Zealand Australia T20 tri‑series venues" or "2017–18 Tri‑nation T20I tournament venues").
  2. "England cricket team in Australia in 2017–18" full Wikipedia or tour page (for links to the tri‑series/venue list).
  3. If needed, pages listing match fixtures/venues (search: "venues list tri‑series 2017–18 Australia New Zealand England").
response.hop2_query: 2017–18 England New Zealand Australia T20 tri‑series venues
hop2_docs: 2017–18 Trans-Tasman Tri-Series | The 2017–18 Trans-Tasman Tri-Series is an upcoming Twenty20 International (T20I) cricket tournament scheduled to be held in Australia and New Zealand in February 2018.  It is a tri-nation series between Australia, England and New Zealand.  It follows on from England's tour of Australia, which includes the 2017–18 Ashes series, and takes the place of the planned Chappell–Hadlee Trophy series.

2017–18 Ashes series | The 2017–18 Ashes series is a forthcoming series of Test cricket matches to be contested between England and Australia for The Ashes.  The series will be played at five venues across Australia between 23 November 2017 and 8 January 2018.

1982–83 Australian Tri-Series | The 1982-83 World Series was a One Day International (ODI) cricket tri-series where Australia played host to England and New Zealand.  Australia and New Zealand reached the Finals, which Australia won 3-0.

1990–91 Australian Tri-Series | The 1990–91 World Series was a One Day International (ODI) cricket tri-series where Australia played host to England and New Zealand.  Australia and New Zealand reached the Finals, which Australia won 2-0.
2008 Beach Cricket Tri-Nations series | The 2008 XXXX Gold Beach Cricket Tri-Nations series was contested between Australia, England and New Zealand, with the eventual winner being New Zealand.

2017 Ireland Tri-Nation Series | The 2017 Ireland Tri-Nation Series was a One Day International cricket tournament that took place in Ireland in May 2017.  It was a tri-nation series between Ireland, Bangladesh and New Zealand.  The matches were in preparation for the 2017 ICC Champions Trophy, which is scheduled to take place in June 2017 in England and Wales.  Cricket Ireland announced the full fixtures in July 2016.  Ahead of the ODI fixtures, Ireland played two warm-up matches; a 50-over match against Bangladesh and a 25-over match against New Zealand.

English cricket team in Australia in 2017–18 | The England cricket team is scheduled to tour Australia between November 2017 and February 2018 to play five Tests and five One Day Internationals (ODIs).  They will also take part in a three nation Twenty20 International (T20I) tournament, along with New Zealand, who will co-host the tournament along with Australia.  The Test series will form part of the 2017–18 Ashes series.  In May 2017, it was confirmed that the WACA Ground would host the Test and ODI in Perth, as the planned new ground was unable to open in time.
hop2_titles: English cricket team in Australia in 2017–18
